records.ety: [OF. escluse, F. cluse, LL. exclusa, sclusa, from L. excludere, exclusum, to shut out: cf. D. sluis sluice, from the Old French. See Exclude.]

defs: An artifical passage for water, fitted with a valve or gate, as in a mill stream, for stopping or regulating the flow; also, a water gate or flood gate.
Hence, an opening or channel through which anything flows; a source of supply.
quotes: Each sluice of affluent fortune opened soon.
This home familiarity . . . opens the sluices of sensibility.

defs: The stream flowing through a flood gate.
A long box or trough through which water flows, -- used for washing auriferous earth.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sluiced (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Sluicing (?).]

defs: To emit by, or as by, flood gates.
To wet copiously, as by opening a sluice; as, to sluice meadows.
quotes: He dried his neck and face, which he had been sluicing with cold water.

defs: To wash with, or in, a stream of water running through a sluice; as, to sluice eart or gold dust in mining.
